title: Gastrointestinal physiology: The stomach & gastrointestinal hormones
body_html:
Functions of the stomach
The primary functions of the stomach are to store and digest food (mechanically and chemically) into chyme. The stomach also performs other important functions such as regulating the flow of chyme from the stomach into the duodenum and producing intrinsic factor to facilitate vitamin B12 absorption.

Stomach cells and gastric pits
The stomach lining has depressions in its surface containing gastric pits which lead into gastric glands. Each gastric pit contains multiple cell types and the cell type may vary depending on which part of the stomach the gastric pit is in. For instance parietal and chief cells are usually only found in gastric pits within the fundus and body of the stomach but not in the pylorus. G-cells, by contrast, are typically only found in gastric pits within the pylorus.

Diagram illustrating typical organisation of a gastric pit. Click on image to enlarge

Cells of the stomach and their secretions
Cell TypeSecretion
Goblet(mucus neck cell)mucus
Chief cellPepsinogen
Parietal cellGastric acid (HCL) and intrinsic factor
G-cellGastrin
D-cellSomatostatin
Enterochromaffin-Like (ECL) Cells
(type of enteroendocrine cell)		Histamine

Gastric fluid
Gastric fluid, or juice, is a combination of the secretions of the different cell types listed above.

Stomach acid
The stomach typically produces around 2L of acid per day. The acid in the stomach is hydrochloric acid (HCL) and is produced by the parietal cells.

The HCL plays an important role in activating pepsinogen into its active form pepsin. Pepsin is an endopeptidase enzyme that breaks down proteins into smaller peptides.


To maintain the acidic environment the parietal cells use a hydrogen-potassium-ATPase which exchanges potassium from the intestinal lumen with cytoplasmic hydrogen ions.

  • The H+/K+ ATPase is the proton pump of the stomach

Protein digestion begins in the stomach when polypeptides are broken down by enzymes called pepsins. Pepsinogen (inactive precursor of pepsin) is converted into pepsin by hydrochloric acid secretd from chief cells in the gastric mucosa.

Protection against stomach acid
The mucosa of the stomach is protected from acid auto-digestion by the secretions of the gastric glands which are alkaline and mucin rich. These secretions form a protective barrier over epithelial cells.

In the event of the cells become irritated local factors stimulate bicarbonate production to neutralise acid and prostaglandins are released which promote mucus layer thickening.

Gastrointestinal hormones
Below is a brief summary of the major hormones involved in food digestion:

SourceStimulusActions
Gastrin
G cells in antrum of the stomach
  • Distension of stomach
  • Vagus nerves (mediated by GRP)
  • Luminal peptides/amino acids
Inhibited by:
  • Low antral pH
  • Somatostatin
  • Secretin
  • GIP (gastric inhibitory peptide)
  • Other hormones: VIP (vasoactive intestinal peptide), glucagon & calcitonin
Increase HCL, pepsinogen and IF secretion, increases gastric motility, stimulates parietal cell maturation
CCK
I cells in upper small intestine
Fatty acids and certain amino acids (products of partially digested proteins and triglycerides)
Increases secretion of enzyme-rich fluid from pancreas, contraction of gallbladder and relaxation of sphincter of Oddi, decreases gastric emptying, trophic effect on pancreatic acinar cells, induces satiety
Secretin
S cells in upper small intestine
Acidic chyme, fatty acids
Increases secretion of bicarbonate-rich fluid from pancreas and hepatic duct cells, decreases gastric acid secretion, delay gastric emptying, trophic effect on pancreatic acinar cells, increases bile formation & stimulates the release of pepsinogen
VIP
Small intestine, pancreas
Neural
Stimulates secretion by pancreas and intestines, inhibits acid secretion
Somatostatin
D cells in the pancreas & stomach
Fat, bile salts and glucose in the intestinal lumen
Inhibited by:
  • vagal stimulation
Decreases acid and pepsin secretion, decreases gastrin secretion, decreases pancreatic enzyme secretion, decreases insulin and glucagon secretion
inhibits trophic effects of gastrin, stimulates gastric mucous production, inhibits CCK release & inhibits gastric emptying

Terms
CCK: cholecystokinin
VIP: vasoactive intestinal peptide
NCBI10
Physiology, Secretin
Gastric Secretion: The Cephalic & Gastric Phase
